name: Reading Sheet Music
description: Learn to read standard music notation from scratch: the staff and clefs, every note on the treble and bass staves, note values and rests, accidentals, key and time signatures, dynamics, articulation, tempo terms, and repeat navigation.

fields.front: How many lines does a standard musical staff have?
fields.extra: Five lines and four spaces. Each line and space is a different pitch, and higher on the staff always means higher in pitch.

fields.front: What are these short lines above and below the staff called?
fields.extra: They extend the staff for notes too high or too low to fit on it. Middle C sits on a ledger line between the treble and bass staves.

fields.front: Two staves joined at the left by a curly symbol: what is the symbol, and what is the pair called?
fields.back: The symbol is a **brace**; the joined treble and bass staves form the **grand staff**
fields.extra: Piano music is written on the grand staff: right hand on the treble staff, left hand on the bass staff.

fields.front: What does this single vertical line through the staff mark?
fields.back: A **bar line**: the boundary between measures (bars)
fields.extra: A measure holds the number of beats set by the time signature.

fields.front: What do these two thin vertical lines mark?
fields.back: A **double bar line**: the end of a section
fields.extra: Two thin lines mark a section; a thin line followed by a thick one marks the end of the piece.

fields.front: What does this thin line followed by a thick line mark?
fields.back: A **final bar line**: the end of the piece
fields.extra: Confusable: two thin lines only mark the end of a section.

fields.front: Which clef is this?
fields.extra: Its curl circles the second line from the bottom, fixing that line as G4. Used for higher-pitched instruments, voices, and the pianist's right hand.

fields.front: Which clef is this?
fields.extra: Its two dots straddle the fourth line from the bottom, fixing that line as F3. Used for lower-pitched instruments and the pianist's left hand.

fields.front: Which clef is this?
fields.extra: Its center points at the middle line, fixing it as middle C. The viola's standard clef.

fields.front: The same pitch is written here in four different clefs. Which note is it?
fields.extra: Treble clef: first ledger line below the staff. Alto and tenor clefs: on the staff. Bass clef: first ledger line above the staff.

fields.text: The treble staff lines, bottom to top, are {{c1::E, G, B, D, F}}, remembered as {{c2::Every Good Boy Does Fine}}.
fields.extra: That is E4, G4, B4, D5, F5. The spaces in between spell FACE.

fields.text: The treble staff spaces, bottom to top, spell the word {{c1::FACE}}.
fields.extra: That is F4, A4, C5, E5.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits on the bottom line (line 1, counting up)?
fields.extra: E4: the *Every* in Every Good Boy Does Fine.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits on line 2 (counting up from the bottom)?
fields.extra: G4: the line the treble clef's curl circles, which is why it is called the G clef.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits on the middle line (line 3)?
fields.extra: B4: the *Boy* in Every Good Boy Does Fine.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits on line 4 (counting up from the bottom)?
fields.extra: D5: the *Does* in Every Good Boy Does Fine.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits on the top line (line 5)?
fields.extra: F5: the *Fine* in Every Good Boy Does Fine.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits in the bottom space (space 1, counting up)?
fields.extra: F4: the F of FACE.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits in space 2 (counting up from the bottom)?
fields.extra: A4: the A of FACE. Concert pitch A440.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits in space 3 (counting up from the bottom)?
fields.extra: C5: the C of FACE, one octave above middle C.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits in the top space (space 4)?
fields.extra: E5: the E of FACE.

fields.front: Treble staff: which note sits on the first ledger line below the staff?
fields.extra: Middle C (C4), one step below the bottom-line E's neighbor D, which hangs just under the staff.

fields.text: The bass staff lines, bottom to top, are {{c1::G, B, D, F, A}}, remembered as {{c2::Good Boys Do Fine Always}}.
fields.extra: That is G2, B2, D3, F3, A3.

fields.text: The bass staff spaces, bottom to top, are {{c1::A, C, E, G}}, remembered as {{c2::All Cows Eat Grass}}.
fields.extra: That is A2, C3, E3, G3.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits on the bottom line (line 1, counting up)?
fields.extra: G2: the *Good* in Good Boys Do Fine Always.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits on line 2 (counting up from the bottom)?
fields.extra: B2: the *Boys* in Good Boys Do Fine Always.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits on the middle line (line 3)?
fields.extra: D3: the *Do* in Good Boys Do Fine Always.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits on line 4 (counting up from the bottom)?
fields.extra: F3: the line the bass clef's two dots straddle, which is why it is called the F clef.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits on the top line (line 5)?
fields.extra: A3: the *Always* in Good Boys Do Fine Always.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits in the bottom space (space 1, counting up)?
fields.extra: A2: the *All* in All Cows Eat Grass.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits in space 2 (counting up from the bottom)?
fields.extra: C3: the *Cows* in All Cows Eat Grass, one octave below middle C.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits in space 3 (counting up from the bottom)?
fields.extra: E3: the *Eat* in All Cows Eat Grass.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits in the top space (space 4)?
fields.extra: G3: the *Grass* in All Cows Eat Grass.

fields.front: Bass staff: which note sits on the first ledger line above the staff?
fields.extra: Middle C (C4): the same note as the first ledger line below the treble staff. The two staves meet at middle C.

fields.front: What note value is this, and how many beats does it get in 4/4 time?
fields.extra: Hollow oval with no stem. British name: semibreve.

fields.front: What note value is this, and how many beats does it get in 4/4 time?
fields.extra: Hollow oval with a stem. British name: minim.

fields.front: What note value is this, and how many beats does it get in 4/4 time?
fields.extra: Filled notehead with a stem. British name: crotchet.

fields.front: What note value is this, and how many beats does it get in 4/4 time?
fields.extra: Filled notehead with a stem and one flag. British name: quaver. Two eighth notes fill one beat.

fields.front: What note value is this, and how many beats does it get in 4/4 time?
fields.back: A **sixteenth note**: a quarter of a beat
fields.extra: Two flags on the stem. British name: semiquaver. Four sixteenth notes fill one beat.

fields.text: Each step down the chain whole, half, quarter, eighth, sixteenth {{c1::halves}} the note's duration.
fields.extra: So one whole note = 2 half notes = 4 quarter notes = 8 eighth notes = 16 sixteenth notes.

fields.text: British note names: whole = {{c1::semibreve}}, half = {{c2::minim}}, quarter = {{c3::crotchet}}, eighth = {{c4::quaver}}, sixteenth = {{c5::semiquaver}}.
fields.extra: You will meet these in UK and Commonwealth method books and exam boards like ABRSM.

fields.front: What is the thick bar joining these note stems, and what does it do to their value?
fields.back: A **beam**: it replaces the flags of eighth (or shorter) notes to group them by beat. The values are unchanged
fields.extra: One beam = eighth notes, two beams = sixteenth notes. Beaming exists purely to make the beat easier to see.

fields.front: What does the dot after this note do to its length?
fields.back: It adds **half the note's own value**: this dotted quarter note lasts 1 1/2 beats
fields.extra: Dotted half = 3 beats, dotted quarter = 1.5 beats, dotted eighth = 0.75 beats.

fields.front: A curved line joins two notes of the **same pitch**. What is it, and how do you play it?
fields.back: A **tie**: play one unbroken note lasting the sum of both values
fields.extra: A quarter tied to a quarter sounds as one half note. Ties let a note cross a bar line. Confusable: the same curve over *different* pitches is a slur.

fields.front: A curved line arches over notes of **different pitches**. What is it, and how do you play them?
fields.back: A **slur**: play the notes legato, smoothly connected without separation
fields.extra: Confusable: the same curve between two notes of the *same* pitch is a tie, which joins them into one note.

fields.front: What rest is this (it hangs below the line), and how long is it?
fields.back: A **whole rest**: 4 beats of silence in 4/4
fields.extra: It also stands for a full measure of rest in any time signature. Mnemonic: the whole rest is heavier, so it *hangs* below the line; the half rest *sits* on top.

fields.front: What rest is this (it sits on top of the line), and how long is it?
fields.extra: Mnemonic: the half rest sits on the middle line like a hat; the heavier whole rest hangs below its line.

fields.front: What rest is this (shown in both its modern and old forms), and how long is it?
fields.extra: The zigzag form is standard today; the mirrored-eighth-rest form appears in older editions.

fields.front: What rest is this, and how long is it?
fields.back: An **eighth rest**: half a beat of silence
fields.extra: One flag on the slanted stroke, matching the eighth note's one flag. The sixteenth rest has two.

fields.front: What rest is this, and how long is it?
fields.back: A **sixteenth rest**: a quarter of a beat of silence
fields.extra: Two flags, matching the sixteenth note's two flags.

fields.front: What is this symbol, and what does it do to a note?
fields.back: A **sharp**: raises the note by a half step (semitone)
fields.extra: F sharp is the black key just above F on a piano.

fields.front: What is this symbol, and what does it do to a note?
fields.back: A **flat**: lowers the note by a half step (semitone)
fields.extra: B flat is the black key just below B on a piano.

fields.front: What is this symbol, and what does it do to a note?
fields.back: A **natural**: cancels a sharp or flat, from the key signature or from earlier in the measure
fields.extra: The note returns to its unaltered (white-key) pitch.

fields.front: What is this x-shaped symbol, and what does it do to a note?
fields.back: A **double sharp**: raises the note by a whole step (two semitones)
fields.extra: F double sharp sounds the same as G. Found mostly in sharp-heavy keys.

fields.front: What is this symbol, and what does it do to a note?
fields.back: A **double flat**: lowers the note by a whole step (two semitones)
fields.extra: B double flat sounds the same as A.

fields.text: An accidental written in front of a note applies to that note for the rest of the {{c1::measure}}, unless cancelled or tied across the bar line.
fields.extra: The next measure resets to the key signature. This is why courtesy accidentals are sometimes printed as reminders.

fields.text: Sharps always appear in key signatures in the order {{c1::F, C, G, D, A, E, B}}, remembered as {{c2::Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle}}.
fields.extra: Each new sharp key adds the next sharp in this fixed order.

fields.text: Flats always appear in key signatures in the order {{c1::B, E, A, D, G, C, F}}, remembered as {{c2::Battle Ends And Down Goes Charles's Father}}.
fields.extra: It is the exact reverse of the sharp order: BEAD plus GCF.

fields.front: Quick trick: how do you name the major key from a sharp key signature?
fields.back: Go up a half step from the **last sharp**
fields.extra: One sharp: F sharp + half step = G major. Four sharps: last is D sharp, so E major.

fields.front: Quick trick: how do you name the major key from a flat key signature?
fields.extra: Three flats (B, E, A): the second-to-last is E flat, so E flat major. The exception with no second-to-last flat is F major (one flat); memorize it.

fields.front: What is a relative minor, and how do you find it from a major key?
fields.back: The minor key that shares the same key signature; its tonic is **three half steps below** the major tonic
fields.extra: C major and A minor both have no sharps or flats. The key signature alone cannot tell you which of the two a piece is in.

fields.front: Which major key has this key signature?
fields.extra: No sharps or flats: all white keys on the piano.

fields.front: Which major key has this key signature?
fields.extra: One sharp: F sharp. Last sharp F sharp + half step = G.

fields.front: Which major key has this key signature?
fields.extra: Two sharps: F sharp and C sharp. Last sharp C sharp + half step = D.

fields.front: Which major key has this key signature?
fields.extra: Three sharps: F, C, and G sharp. Last sharp G sharp + half step = A.

fields.front: Which major key has this key signature?
fields.extra: Four sharps: F, C, G, and D sharp. Last sharp D sharp + half step = E.

fields.front: Which major key has this key signature?
fields.extra: One flat: B flat. The one flat key you must memorize, since the second-to-last-flat trick needs at least two flats.

fields.front: Which major key has this key signature?
fields.extra: Two flats: B flat and E flat. The second-to-last flat, B flat, names the key.

fields.front: Which major key has this key signature?
fields.extra: Three flats: B, E, and A flat. The second-to-last flat, E flat, names the key.

fields.front: Which major key has this key signature?
fields.extra: Four flats: B, E, A, and D flat. The second-to-last flat, A flat, names the key.

fields.front: In a time signature, what does the **top** number tell you?
fields.back: How many beats are in each measure
fields.extra: 3/4 means three beats per measure; 4/4 means four.

fields.front: In a time signature, what does the **bottom** number tell you?
fields.back: Which note value counts as one beat
fields.extra: 4 = quarter note, 8 = eighth note, 2 = half note. So 6/8 means six eighth-note beats' worth per measure.

fields.front: Read this time signature: how is a measure counted?
fields.extra: Waltz time: ONE two three, ONE two three.

fields.front: What time signature does this C-shaped symbol stand for?
fields.extra: The C is not short for common; it survives from the broken circle of medieval mensural notation.

fields.front: What time signature does this symbol (a C with a vertical slash) stand for?
fields.back: **Cut time** (alla breve): 2/2, two half-note beats per measure
fields.extra: Common in marches and fast pieces: the same notes as 4/4 but felt in two.

fields.front: How is 6/8 time actually felt, and how does it differ from 3/4?
fields.back: In two: each measure has **two dotted-quarter beats**, each splitting into three eighth notes (ONE-and-a TWO-and-a)
fields.extra: 6/8 is compound duple; 3/4 is simple triple (three quarter beats). Same six eighth notes per measure, grouped 3+3 versus 2+2+2.

fields.front: What does this marking tell the performer?
fields.back: The tempo: play **120 quarter notes per minute**
fields.extra: A metronome (BPM) marking. The note value shown is the beat unit; in 6/8 you will often see a dotted quarter instead.

fields.title: The standard Italian tempo terms, from slowest to fastest

fields.front: What does the tempo marking **Largo** mean?
fields.extra: Roughly 40-60 BPM. Italian for *wide*.

fields.front: What does the tempo marking **Adagio** mean?
fields.extra: Roughly 66-76 BPM. Faster than largo, slower than andante.

fields.front: What does the tempo marking **Andante** mean?
fields.back: At a walking pace, moderately slow
fields.extra: Roughly 76-108 BPM. From the Italian *andare*, to walk.

fields.front: What does the tempo marking **Moderato** mean?
fields.extra: Roughly 108-120 BPM, between andante and allegro.

fields.front: What does the tempo marking **Allegro** mean?
fields.extra: Roughly 120-156 BPM. Italian for *cheerful*.

fields.front: What does the tempo marking **Vivace** mean?
fields.extra: Roughly 156-176 BPM, between allegro and presto. Italian for *lively*.

fields.front: What does the tempo marking **Presto** mean?
fields.extra: Roughly 168-200 BPM. Prestissimo, even faster, is the top of the scale.

fields.front: What does **rit.** (ritardando) tell you to do?
fields.extra: Rallentando (rall.) means much the same. Confusable: ritenuto means immediately held back, not gradual.

fields.front: What does **accel.** (accelerando) tell you to do?
fields.extra: The opposite of ritardando.

fields.front: After a ritardando, the score says **a tempo**. What do you do?
fields.extra: Literally *in time*: it cancels any accelerando or ritardando.

fields.front: What is this dynamic marking, and how loud is it?
fields.extra: Softer than p. Composers occasionally stack more, like ppp.

fields.front: What is this dynamic marking, and how loud is it?
fields.extra: The instrument is named after the dynamic: the pianoforte could play both soft and loud.

fields.front: What is this dynamic marking, and how loud is it?
fields.extra: Mezzo means half or medium. Louder than p, softer than mf.

fields.front: What is this dynamic marking, and how loud is it?
fields.extra: The default middle ground of most music. Softer than f, louder than mp.

fields.front: What is this dynamic marking, and how loud is it?
fields.extra: Italian for *strong*.

fields.front: What is this dynamic marking, and how loud is it?
fields.extra: Louder than f. The full ladder, soft to loud: pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff.

fields.front: What does this hairpin that opens to the right mean?
fields.extra: Also written cresc. Mnemonic: the shape grows from a point, like the volume.

fields.front: What does this hairpin that closes to a point mean?
fields.extra: Also written dim. or decresc. The mirror image of a crescendo.

fields.front: What does this marking mean?
fields.back: **sfz** (sforzando): a sudden, forceful accent on that one note or chord
fields.extra: From Italian *sforzare*, to force. Related: fp (fortepiano) means loud, then instantly soft.

fields.front: What does the dot above or below this notehead mean?
fields.back: **Staccato**: play the note short and detached
fields.extra: Confusable: a dot *beside* a notehead lengthens it by half; a dot *above or below* shortens its sound.

fields.front: What does this wedge above the note mean?
fields.extra: An exaggerated staccato.

fields.front: What does this > symbol above the note mean?
fields.back: An **accent**: attack the note harder, with extra emphasis
fields.extra: Confusable: the vertical wedge ^ (marcato) is an even stronger accent.

fields.front: What does this horizontal line above or below the notehead mean?
fields.back: **Tenuto**: hold the note for its full value, with a slight lean on it
fields.extra: The opposite spirit of staccato: no daylight after the note.

fields.front: What does this arc with a dot above the note mean?
fields.back: A **fermata**: hold the note longer than its written value, at the performer's discretion
fields.extra: Nicknamed the bird's eye. On a bar line it marks a pause between sections.

fields.front: What do these two dots next to the double bar tell you?
fields.back: A **repeat sign**: go back and play the passage again
fields.extra: Dots facing left end the repeated passage; a mirrored sign with dots facing right marks where it begins. With no start sign, repeat from the beginning.

fields.front: What do these numbered brackets over the final measures of a repeated section mean?
fields.back: **First and second endings** (volta brackets): play ending 1, repeat the section, then skip ending 1 and play ending 2
fields.extra: From Italian *volta*, time: first time, second time.

fields.front: What does **D.C.** (da capo) tell you to do?
fields.back: Go back to the very beginning of the piece
fields.extra: Italian for *from the head*. D.C. al Fine: from the beginning, stop at the word Fine.

fields.front: What does **D.S.** (dal segno) tell you to do?
fields.back: Go back to the segno sign (a stylized S with a slash and dots), not the beginning
fields.extra: Confusable with D.C., which returns to the very start. D.S. al Coda: from the sign, then jump to the coda at the To Coda mark.

fields.front: What is this crosshair-and-circle symbol called, and what is it for?
fields.back: A **coda** sign: it marks the tail section that ends the piece after a D.S. al Coda or D.C. al Coda jump
fields.extra: Italian for *tail*. You jump to it from the To Coda mark, never on the first pass.

fields.front: The score says **D.C. al Fine**. What is the road map?
fields.back: Return to the beginning and play until the word **Fine**, which is where the piece ends
fields.extra: Fine is Italian for *end*. Repeats inside the restated passage are normally not taken the second time.

fields.front: What is this tiny note with a slash through its stem, and how is it played?
fields.back: An **acciaccatura** (crushed grace note): squeezed in as fast as possible just before the main note
fields.extra: Confusable: a small note *without* the slash is an appoggiatura, which takes a real share of the main note's time.

fields.front: What does this **tr** marking tell you to do?
fields.back: Play a **trill**: rapidly alternate between the written note and the note a step above it
fields.extra: Often extended with a wavy line showing how long the trill lasts.

fields.front: What does this wavy vertical line in front of a chord mean?
fields.back: **Arpeggiate** the chord: roll the notes one after another, normally from the bottom up, instead of together
fields.extra: From Italian *arpeggiare*, to play like a harp.

fields.front: What does the small 3 over this group of notes mean?
fields.back: A **triplet**: play three notes in the time normally taken by two of the same value
fields.extra: Three triplet eighth notes fill one quarter-note beat: count *tri-pl-et*.

fields.front: What does this line connecting two distant notes tell you to do?
fields.back: Play a **glissando**: slide continuously from the first pitch to the second
fields.extra: Abbreviated gliss. On piano it is a sweep across the keys; on trombone or strings, a true continuous slide.

fields.front: A dashed line labeled **8va** runs above a passage. How do you play it?
fields.extra: 8vb (or 8va below the staff) means an octave lower. Used to avoid towers of ledger lines.
